turns.text: For the time being I have no further questions to put to this witness.

turns.speaker: DR. EGON KUBUSCHOK (Counsel for Defendant Von Papen )
turns.text: Witness, are you well acquainted with Von Papen as a result of the period during which you were working in the Foreign Office and particularly during the time you were active as State Secretary in the Foreign Office?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I had known Herr Von Papen for several years before 1933, but privately. Then I lost track of him for some time and re-established contact with him when I became State Secretary in the German Foreign Office. Then I was continually associated with him in an official and unofficial capacity.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Did you, particularly in the last period of your activities as State Secretary, continually receive the reports which Von Papen, as Ambassador in Ankara, sent to Berlin?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Unless Herr Von Papen sent reports directly to Von Ribbentrop -- which may have been possible; I do not know -- I received them weekly through official channels.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Do you remember that after two previous refusals Von Papen took over the post of Ambassador in Ankara, in April 1939, on the day that Italy occupied Albania, whereby an acute danger of war arose in the Southeast?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: At that time I was not State Secretary and also had no political position, so that I am not acquainted with the events of that period. But today I have the impression that he took over that position after the Italians had occupied Albania. And he himself told me later that at that time there was danger that the Italians would advance further into the Balkans, possibly causing a conflict with Turkey, as a result of which world peace would have been endangered. For that reason he had decided at the time to accept the post. Exactly on which day that was, I cannot say.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: What can you say in general about Herr Von Papen's efforts toward peace?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I am under the impression that Herr Von Papen always strove to preserve peace by every means. He certainly considered that it would be a great disaster for Germany and the world if war were to break out.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Were the efforts which Von Papen made during the war towards establishing peace aimed at foregoing any annexations regardless of the military outcome and completely reestablishing the sovereignty of occupied territories, in short, to achieve, by means of reasonable renunciation, a bearable status for all European states?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: In principle it was quite clear that Von Papen always worked for the re-establishment of peace under conditions which would have re-established full sovereignty for all countries, and so that no encroachment nor damage, material or otherwise would be inflicted on any foreign countries.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Was that Von Papen's attitude even at the time of the greatest German military successes?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I believe that his basic attitude in this respect never changed.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Were his continuous personal efforts to establish peace held against Von Papen by Hitler, and was he considered a disagreeable outsider in that connection?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I did not have an opportunity to discuss it with Hitler; I only know that he was quite generally criticized by Hitler and other persons as a man who always followed a weak line.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Did Herr Von Papen frankly acknowledge that peace would be impossible as long as Hitler and the Party existed in Germany and the necessary credit for negotiating abroad was lacking?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, I think it must have been about April 1943 or May 1943, that I spoke to Von Papen in detail about the whole subject, since, at that time, I had just become State Secretary. At that time he very clearly voiced the opinion to me which you have just sketched. It was quite plain to him that foreign countries would conclude no peace with Hitler and the methods he employed.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Just one last question, Witness: The Indictment accuses the Defendant Von Papen of being an unscrupulous opportunist. You, Witness, know the defendant from the reports and from all the official relations the defendant had with his superior office for a number of years. Did you, on the strength of that knowledge, get the impression that this characterization of Von Papen is correct, or can you say, on the strength of these reports and these official relations, that Von Papen appears to you to be a man who always tells the truth, even when that truth is disagreeable to his quite unpleasant superiors, and even when the voicing of that truth involves personal danger for him?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I can say that is absolutely so. I find the best evidence of it is that Herr Von Papen was finally completely eliminated from the position of Vice-Chancellor and resigned from the government, then he became a private citizen and only in the greatest emergency was he called upon. In my opinion, Von Papen made himself available only because he said to himself, "I have still got a certain amount of credit, I am a good Catholic, and accordingly I represent an attitude which is opposed to all inhumanity, et cetera . Perhaps I can, through my intervention, exercise some influence in that direction." I myself never attended a meeting or a conference which took place between Hitler and Von Papen, but, particularly from my liaison officer with Hitler, I often heard that Von Papen, in his smooth way, often told Hitler many things which no one else could have told Hitler and I believe that through his manner he prevented a number of things, at least for a time.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.speaker: DR. OTTO NELTE (Counsel for Defendant Keitel )
turns.text: Witness, you have stated that Hitler, because of the terrible bombing attack on Dresden, intended to issue an order according to which thousands of prisoners of war were to be killed in reprisal.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Do I remember your testimony of yesterday correctly, that all you have said about this matter is information from, or based on information from Herr Von Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: What do you know from your own personal knowledge?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: From my own personal knowledge I only know that our liaison man with Hitler called me on the telephone and told me that Goebbels had proposed to Hitler that 10,000 or more British and American prisoners of war be shot in reprisal, and that Hitler would agree or had agreed. I immediately reported this to Von Ribbentrop, and he went there at once and told me after half an hour that the order had been withdrawn. About Field Marshal Keitel I know nothing at all in that connection.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: You do not know, therefore, who was the originator of that order?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Who suggested it, I mean.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: The suggestion for that order evidently came from Goebbels according to the information which I received.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Through Herr Von Ribbentrop, do you mean?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Who?

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Through Herr Von Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No, Von Ribbentrop had nothing to do with that.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Then from Herr Hewel?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Herr Hewel told me that. He called me up and told me that.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: And you know nothing about the participation of military men?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I know nothing at all about the participation of military men.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Thank you very much. DR. HANS LATERNSER (Counsel for General Staff and High Command of the German Armed Forces): Witness, I have only one question. Did you, as State Secretary, or did the Foreign Office regularly inform military offices, for instance, the Army High Command or the High Command of the Navy, with reference to pertinent matters of German politics?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No, they were not informed.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: I have no further questions.

turns.speaker: THE PRESEDENT
turns.text: Does the British Prosecutor wish to crossexamine?

turns.speaker: COLONEL H. J. PHILLIMORE (Junior Counsel for the United Kingdom)
turns.text: Witness, you told us yesterday that the Defendant Ribbentrop was against the persecution of the churches, was against the persecution of Jews, and did not know what was going on in the concentration camps. You have told us that he was not a typical Nazi. What are the qualities of a typical Nazi?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: By a typical National Socialist, I mean a man who fanatically acknowledges and represents all the doctrines of National Socialism. Herr Von Ribbentrop, as I said, followed Hitler personally, but he really knew uncommonly little of any of the other ideology and never bothered about it. He never spoke at meetings, never participated in large rallies, and therefore, he really knew extremely little about the people and the mood of the people.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: By "a typical Nazi," do you mean someone who was persecuting the churches?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I did not understand that question.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I will repeat it. By "a typical Nazi," do you mean a man who was engaged in persecution of the churches?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: At any rate, someone who, if Adolf Hitler considered it right, did not state his personal opinion on the matter.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And a man who would take his full share in persecution and extermination of Jews?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: That I would not like to say either. That was limited to a certain circle of people. A large number even of fanatical Nazis knew nothing about these atrocities and repudiated them and would have repudiated them, had they been properly informed of them.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I understand you to say that you knew nothing of them yourself. Is that so?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: That I knew nothing?

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: In my position as State Secretary and because I read foreign papers, and particularly since I had contact with the opposition, I knew of many things connected with concentration camps. In all these cases, as far as it was in my power, I intervened. But regarding the things which I have heard here now, I knew nothing at all.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Now, I want to ask you about another matter. You have told us that Ribbentrop had no responsibility in the occupied territories. Your words were that "the Foreign Office lost responsibility at that moment at which the German bayonet crossed the frontier." Is that right?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I said that at that moment at which the German bayonet crossed the frontier the Foreign Office lost the sole right to negotiate with foreign governments everywhere. Beyond that, in most countries, the Foreign Office did not have the right to have even a diplomatic observer without authority, particularly in Norway and the Eastern Territories.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You have said the Foreign Office had no right to have an observer there, and that direct relations with occupied territories were withdrawn, is that right?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No, I said that in all occupied territories the Foreign Office no longer had the sole right to negotiate with the government, since there was then either a civil administration in those countries or a military government with auxiliary command offices and a military administrative head, and that these offices themselves then approached the foreign governments and their executive organs in the countries occupied at that time. Consequently one can no longer say that the Foreign Office had the sole right to negotiate with the governments. But in some countries, as in the North and the East, we no longer had any of our people at all, and Hitler had issued the order that we withdraw our observers from the other countries, such as Holland, Belgium and so on. However, we did not do so.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You say that in France you had an ambassador reporting direct to Ribbentrop, did you not?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And his duties included advising the Secret Field Police and the Secret State Police by the impounding of politically important documents and securing and seizure of public property; further, of private and, above all, Jewish artistic property on the basis of instructions especially given for the matter. Isn't that right?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I already emphasized yesterday that only since 1943 had I anything at all to do with political affairs. If I understood your question correctly, Mr. Prosecutor, you are of the opinion that the Secret State Police and the German executive organs in France were under our jurisdiction. That is incorrect.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You are not answering the question. I asked you if the Minister Abetz had not got those duties.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: He did not have the assignment of confiscating any French property or carrying out any action against the Jews. No orders of that kind went through my hands during my time, and he could ...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: [Handing the document to the witness.] Will you look at Document 3614-PS. My Lord, that was put in as French Exhibit Number RF-1061 on the 4th of February. It is a letter dated the 3rd of August 1940, signed by Ribbentrop, to the Chief of the Supreme Command of the Armed Forces (OKW). It reads: then it sets out a number of tasks and Number 6 is the one I put to the witness: Then the concluding paragraphs: I want to ask you one or two questions about the Jews. You have told us that you and the Defendant Ribbentrop...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Phillimore, the Tribunal would like to know why this witness told them that Ambassador Abetz did not have the task of confiscating property. [Turning to the witness.] Why did you say that?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Ambassador Abetz had no executive powers, and he was expressly forbidden to intervene in French internal affairs. He could therefore, address himself exclusively to the French Government, and if the French Government did anything by means of their executive power, then that was a transaction on the part of the French Government but never a confiscation carried out by Abetz.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: That is not an answer to the question. The question is why, when you were asked whether Abetz had the task of advising the Secret Field Police and the Secret State Police on the impounding of politically important documents, did you not say so?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I said that no order went through my hands, since I did not become State Secretary until May 1943. This is an order of 3 August 1940. But here we are concerned only with an official directive addressed to Ambassador Abetz.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: At this time you were Ribbentrop's personal adjutant, weren't you?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I was adjutant, but not political secretary. I was only ...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You were adjutant?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I was adjutant, that is to say I was concerned with technical matters. At that time I never presented a political report to him. But I should add, if I may, this concerns a directive to Ambassador Abetz and this directive was completely outdated by actual conditions. Because advising the Secret Field Police ...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: How do you know that, if you were only personal adjutant and not acting in political matters?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Ambassador Abetz was ambassador until May 1945. Therefore from 1943 to 1945 I continuously corresponded with him, and during that time Ambassador Abetz continually fought against the measures which were carried out by the Secret State Police anyway. It was a bitter struggle and he was personally threatened in all possible matters. One can talk about advice, but whether people heeded him -- he had no power -- that is quite another question.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Does it come to this, that your answer about occupied territories applies only after 1943?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: From my own experience I can speak only about the period after 1943.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Now, I want to turn to the question of Jews. You have told us that you and Ribbentrop, by adopting a policy of delay, prevented the holding of the Anti-Jewish Congress in 1944; is that correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And that you were against the policy of persecution of the Jews.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And so was the Defendant Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I want you to look at Document 3319-PS. [Handing the document to the witness.] My Lord, that is a new document. It will be Exhibit GB-287. [Turning to witness.] Now you have got a photostat there. Will you look at Page 4 of the German -- that's the first page of the English. That is a letter dated the 28th of April on the subject of anti-Jewish action in foreign countries. It is marked at the bottom of Page 4.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I have not found it.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Will you look at Page 4, marked in a black square at the bottom of the page. You see a letter dated the 28th of April 1944, Subject: Anti-Jewish action in foreign countries, and it is addressed to practically every German legation and mission abroad.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Turn to Page 10. You will see that it purports to be signed by you; is that correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You remember the letter? I will read you the first paragraph to refresh your memory. "The Reich Foreign Minister... "

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Then you set out the co-workers, number of departments of the Foreign Office, and then one permanent representative of the Reich Security Main Office -- that's Himmler's office, isn't it?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And one representative of the office of Reichsleiter Rosenberg. That department just up above "Inland II," that is the Foreign Office which had liaison with the SS, isn't it?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: At that time the chief was a man called Wagner and the assistant chief, Von Thadden?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Do you still say that you were against the policy of persecution of the Jews?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, I maintain that now as before. I also say, as I have already said during earlier interrogations, that even the holding of an anti-Jewish congress in its effect would not have been directed against the Jews because what was happening in Germany was all taking place under the seal of secrecy and no one was informed in any way. The Jews disappeared. But if there had been an international congress, one would have been forced in the first place to bring up the question: where are these Jews anyway? What is actually happening to these Jews?

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Is the point this, that you wanted to put off an anti-Jewish congress because that would be known to the world, but you were quite prepared to set up an organization in the Foreign Office?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Gentlemen, we must separate two completely different problems here. The one problem is this: There were offices in Germany which conducted and carried out anti-Jewish measures. These organizations also reached abroad and there, without the knowledge and without the participation of the Foreign Office, did away with the people in foreign countries. Consequently, an improvement and a policy guided to some extent into normal channels could exist only if some German department had really assumed responsibility for these things at that time. For we did not hear of these matters; we always heard the complaints which we received from foreign mission heads about events which took place. But we had no means of control. If I applied to the inner German offices ...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Was this set up to control the anti-Jewish policy, this department?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Apparently we are discussing two different matters here today. The anti-Jewish congress had been ordered. The fact that Rosenberg's office was holding an anti-Jewish congress ...

turns.speaker: THE PRESEDENT
turns.text: You are not answering the question. The question was: Was this organization, referred to in this letter, set up to control the organization of anti-Jewish work abroad? That is the question. Can you not answer that by "yes" or "no"?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: The Foreign Office could not exercise general control since all anti-Jewish questions were principally dealt with in Rosenberg's office.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Well then, what was the purpose of this organization of the Foreign Office?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: By Hitler's order we had to contact all German departments and archives in order to collect all the material there, and we attached importance ...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And this was ordered by Ribbentrop, wasn't it?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: As set out in your letter?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes. And we thought it important that we get an idea in this way of what was actually happening to the Jews, et cetera , and therefore we drew in people from all offices.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I will show you in a minute what was actually happening and out of your own files, but I just want to put this to you: The point of your putting off the anti-Jewish congress was simply because you did not want the world to know. You had not the slightest objection to setting up an anti-Jewish organization in Germany. Now, will you look at Page 32 of the German text. My Lord, that is on Page 23 of the English text. You will see there a letter from Rosenberg's office to the Foreign Office, signed by Brautigam, Page 32 of the German text. It is marked at the bottom of Page 32. Brautigam was your liaison officer with Rosenberg, wasn't he, Witness? Was Brautigam your liaison officer in Rosenberg's office?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No. Brautigam was, I think, in the Foreign Office in 1941.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And in 1942.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, but in 1941, since he had previously been working on Eastern problems in the Foreign Office, he had been transferred and was now in the Rosenberg office.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Very well. And you will see there he is referring to a conference with ObersturmbannFuehrer Eichmann, that is, the chief of the Jewish section of the Gestapo, and a Dr. Wetzel, and he sends you a copy of an agreement made at Tighina in Romania on the 30th of August 1941 with the request for acknowledgment.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Mr. Prosecutor, there could be an error here. This letter is dated 11 March 1942. I became State Secretary in May 1943. I therefore know nothing about this matter. I should like to remark ...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You just listen and wait until you are asked a question. We shall get on faster if you just listen to the letter: Now, that enclosed an agreement made between the German and Romanian General Staffs, and, if you will look at Paragraph 7, on Page 38 of the German, Page 27 of the English, this was the agreement they made: And then there's a note on the file on the next page of the German, still on Page 27 of the English: Now, do you doubt that that agreement, enclosed with that letter sent to the Foreign Office, would have reached the Defendant Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Well. I see this document and this agreement for the first time today. Nothing of this entire affair...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Yes. Would you answer the question? Do you doubt that that letter and that agreement enclosed with it would have been shown to the Defendant Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: At that time there was an Under Secretary of State Luther in the Foreign Office who acted quite independently; and I fought a bitter battle against him although I was not called upon to do it, because he wanted to introduce National Socialist methods. Whether he submitted this matter to Ribbentrop or not I cannot decide.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Very well. We come to a time when you were the State Secretary. Would you look at Page 31 of the German text, Page 20 of the English.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What do the words that follow the passage you have just read mean on Page 27: "Bucharest, 17 October 1941 (Signature illegible)" -- and below -- "To be discussed with Vice Minister President Antonescu. Confidential, Bucharest, 16 October 1943"?

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: My Lord, it is badly typed. "Bucharest, 17 October 1943" and then follows the next letter. The previous part is a note on the file.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: It is a note on the German Legation file on Bucharest.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Go on.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I have not troubled the Tribunal with the following letters. They deal with the earlier date on the expulsion of Jews from firms owned by citizens of the German Reich. [Turning to the witness.] Now would you look at Page 31 of the German, Page 20 of the English. You will see there a document sent to ...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: When you started that document you didn't give the date in full. The year there appears to be 1944, doesn't it?

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: It is not. In 1942, I think, My Lord.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: It should be April 29, 1942? Is the date at the head of the document?

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: My Lord, the letter I read was dated March '42 and marked with a foreign office stamp "Received 13th of March 1942..."

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I am speaking of the whole document, Page 1 of the document.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: My Lord, it is a file, one of those rather inconvenient documents, a file, and it starts with the earliest date at the bottom and then works up to 1944.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, then the part you read first ...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: That was 1944.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well. What page are you going to now?

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I was going to Page 20 now, My Lord. [Turning to the witness.] Now, this is a communication from Von Thadden who was, as you have told us, assistant in the Department Inland II, to the German Legation in Bucharest. It is dated 12 October 1943, and it is stamped as received on 18 October. And he encloses a letter signed by Muller in the Reichssicherheitshauptamt, to all German police authorities abroad. You will see that it goes to the commander of the Security Police in Prague, The Hague, Paris, Brussels, Metz, Strasbourg, Luxembourg, Krakow, Kiev, Smolensk, and so on. October '43. That is after you had become Secretary of State, isn't it?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You were appointed in April?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Turning to the substance of the letter, the subject is the treatment of Jews with foreign citizenship in the sphere of German power: And then there are arrangements about baggage. And if you look at 31-e, you will see at the foot of Page 22, on the English, that that had been signed by Muller and then was signed again by a clerk of Himmler's office. And then on the next page of the English, still on 31-e of the German, Himmler's office sends it to the Foreign Office, to Von Thadden, on 2 October. Now, did you not see that document when it got to the Foreign Office?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No, I see this document today for the first time.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You were the State Secretary?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes. This obviously concerns a measure which was ordered by another office. Within the German Reich the Foreign Office had no executive powers at all and no possibilities and consequently...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: No executive powers, but it was sent to you for information.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: That was sent to us, this affair, solely for our information, and it was not given to me, this affair.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You had a departmental liaison with the SS, a Mr. Von Thadden. Was he not a competent official?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: The exact content of this affair I do not even know now, because I have not read it through at leisure. I can imagine only the following in reference to this whole matter: The question whether the Jews who were in Germany could be returned to their home countries was discussed for a long time. This, I think, is what we are concerned with here?

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I don't think we are interested in your imagination. Either you know or do not know. I asked you whether Von Thadden was a competent official.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I have not seen this document.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You are not answering the question. Was Von Thadden a competent official?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Von Thadden was a man from the Foreign Office who knew his job.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Yes, knew his job. And do you not think that as State Secretary he ought to have shown you this document?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: He should have done that, certainly, if this matter was not arranged in another office, and I was completely excluded from the anti-Jewish action. Also instructions about anti-Jewish actions abroad never went through my office. I pointed out yesterday, at the beginning of my statement, that many matters were arranged directly in the highest places, and that the Foreign Office also was not notified afterwards, and orders in these matters...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: This is a document you were informed about?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Muller sent it to the Foreign Office.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And you sent it to your legation at Bucharest?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: He ought certainly to have put that before me. But I did not see it.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And if you just look again at the letter, you notice how Muller's instructions start. He begins, "In agreement with the Foreign Office..."

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Where does it say so? Unfortunately I have not found it.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: At the start of the letter: "Subject: Treatment of Jews of foreign citizenship in the sphere of German power." And then he begins: "In agreement with the Foreign Office..." Does that just mean in agreement with Mr. Von Thadden?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I assume that this type of thing went to the competent experts, and since this concerns a basic matter it was put directly before Herr Von Ribbentrop. I request that Herr Von Ribbentrop should be asked whether he knows of this matter or not. I have not seen this matter.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: This is a matter of such importance that it could not have been agreed with the Foreign Office without Ribbentrop being consulted; isn't that the case?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: In my opinion, I would never have decided alone on this matter if it had been put before me. I am of the opinion that it was an affair which would have to be put before Von Ribbentrop.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Good. And, of course, Von Ribbentrop was one of the most ruthless persecutors of Jews, wasn't he?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: That is not correct.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I am going to read you a short passage from a conference between the Fuehrer, Ribbentrop and the Hungarian Regent, Horthy. This is Document D-736, which was put in as Exhibit GB-283 by Sir David Maxwell-Fyfe, to the Defendant Goering. This was a meeting at Klesshelm Castle on the morning of 17 of April 1943. And you see the minutes are signed by Schmidt.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PBILLIMORE
turns.text: The question of Jews was raised: And then, you see, the Fuehrer goes on to describe them as tuberculosis bacilli. Now, in the face of that document, do you still say that the Defendant Ribbentrop was against the policy of persecution and extermination of the Jews?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I said yesterday already that Herr Von Ribbentrop, when he was with Hitler ...

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Never mind what you said yesterday. I am putting it to you now, today. You have now seen that document. Do you still say that Ribbentrop was against the policy of persecution and extermination of the Jews?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Here, too, I should like to make a distinction between the real instincts of Von Ribbentrop and what he said when he was under Hitler's influence. I said already yesterday that he was completely hypnotized by Hitler and then became his tool.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Yes, became his tool. And from then on, he was prepared to do anything that Hitler wanted and was as violent a Nazi as anyone; isn't that right?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: He followed blindly the orders given by Hitler.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Yes. And to the extent of conniving at any and every atrocity, isn't that right?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Since he had no executive powers he personally did not commit these cruelties.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do any of the other chief prosecutors want to cross-examine?

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: You testified yesterday that you did not consider Ribbentrop to be a typical Nazi; is that correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Do you consider Goering to be a typical Nazi?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Goering made speeches at every type of meeting and fought for the seizure of power, and accordingly he had a completely different position in the party than Ribbentrop.

turns.speaker: COL.AMEN
turns.text: I think you can answer my question "yes" or "no." We are trying to save time as much as possible.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, certainly.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Do you consider Goering to be a typical Nazi according to the same standards that you were using with Ribbentrop yes or no?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: This question one cannot answer in that way with "yes" or "no." I am trying every...

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: You answered it that way with respect to Ribbentrop, didn't you?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Goering was a peculiar type of person. I cannot class him with the ordinary Nazis, as one usually expresses it.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: In other words, you don't know whether you think he is a typical Nazi or not, is that what you want the Tribunal to understand?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: By a typical Nazi one understands the "average" Nazi. Goering is a unique person and one cannot compare his manner of living with the other National Socialists.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, are you acquainted with all of the gentlemen in the box there in front of you?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Now, will you tell me which of those individuals you consider to be a typical Nazi, according to the standards which you applied yesterday to Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Amen, I do not want to interrupt your cross-examination, but want to say that there is too much laughter and noise in Court, and I cannot have it. Go on, Colonel; with your cross-examination.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Do you understand my last question? Please name those of the defendants in the box whom you consider to be typical Nazis, on the same standard which you yesterday applied to Ribbentrop.

turns.speaker: DR. HORN
turns.text: Mr. President, I am convinced that here the witness is making a decision which in my opinion should be made by the Court at the end of the proceedings. That is an evaluation which the witness cannot make.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: This is the subject that was brought up by this very Counsel yesterday with respect to Ribbentrop.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal thinks it a perfectly proper question. They understand that the phrase "a typical Nazi" was used by the witness himself.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And please just give us the names and not a long explanation, if you can.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I said yesterday that by "typical Nazi" I meant people who are familiar with the dogma and doctrine. I want to add today that by "typical Nazis" I mean further those people who during the time of struggle represented National Socialist ideology and were propagandists of National Socialism. Rosenberg's book is known, Herr Frank, as President of the Academy for German Law is known, these are really -- Hess, of course, too -- and these are people whom I want to put into the foreground very particularly because by their writings and so forth and by their speeches they became known. No one ever heard Ribbentrop make an election speech.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: But you are not answering my question. Am I to assume from that that in your opinion Rosenberg, Frank and Hess are the only persons whom you could characterize as being typical Nazis, according to your standards?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Well, shall I go through the ranks of the defendants to give an opinion on each one?

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Precisely. Just give me the names. No, I do not want your opinion. I want to know under your standards which of them you consider to be typical Nazis.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I have already stated the standard before. It can be proved by whether the people unreservedly represented the National Socialist ideology in words or at meetings and in this respect I named the prominent ones.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And you consider all of the others not to be typical Nazis? Correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I did not say that. Then I would have to go through them individually.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: I have asked you to do that three times. Will you please name them individually?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I also see Herr Sauckel . Herr Sauckel was Gauleiter and was active as a leader in the National Socialist movement. Then I see the Reich Youth Leader, who educated the Hitler Youth.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Who else? Just give me the names. Do not give these explanations, please.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Well, I think that with that I have pointed out the typical representatives of the Party.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, how about Streicher?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I do not see him here, or I would have answered in the affirmative.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: In other words, you consider him to be a typical Nazi under your standards?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, but please do not attribute his abuses to all National Socialists.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Now, while you were working with Ribbentrop, do I understand that you knew nothing about the murders, tortures, starvations and killings which were taking place in the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: By the fact that foreign diplomats applied to me, and by the fact that I was informed by opposition elements in Germany, and from enemy propaganda, I knew of the existence and some of the methods. But, I emphasize, only a part of the methods. I learned about the total extent and degree only in internment here.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Did you know that priests were being tortured and starved and killed in concentration camps while you were working with Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No, I heard nothing specific regarding individual things that occurred there, and if that had happened or has happened to priests, then I would consider the only authentic information to be that which the Nuncio or the Vatican had given me; but that did not occur. But in spite of the fact that, as I said yesterday, the Vatican had no jurisdiction, I took care of all cases based on humanity, that is, all humanitarian cases. I took care of them, and always strove to handle them successfully. I handled 87 cases in which my activity threatened to bring about my death. I intervened in hundreds of cases, and thus saved, or at least improved, the lives of thousands and thousands of people.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: If you don't confine your answers directly to my questions, it is very difficult to get through and to save time. Now, will you please try to answer my questions "yes" or "no," if possible, and make your explanations short. Do you understand?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I understand perfectly. As far as I can, I shall of course do so.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Did you know that nuns were being tortured and starved and killed in concentrations camps, while you were working with Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: You did not know either about what was happening to priests or the nuns or to other inmates of concentrations camps? Correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I have just said that I have intervened in hundreds of cases, in which I was approached by the Nuncio even when it concerned Jews, for whom the Nuncio was not authorized to act, and in cases in which the Nuncio was acting on behalf of Polish clergymen, also a sphere for which he was not authorized. In spite of the fact that I had strictest orders not to receive such cases, I did receive the cases; and, in spite of the "Nacht und Nebel" decree, I always gave information when I could get any information. Details other than those which I received officially I did not have.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And who gave you the instructions not to do anything about these complaints?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: These orders came directly from Hitler and came to me through Ribbentrop.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How do you know?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I have already said yesterday that the two notes which before my time were passed by State Secretary Von Weizsacker to Hitler through Ribbentrop were rejected with the remarks that they were blunt lies and, apart from that, this was not within the jurisdiction of the Nuncio; these notes were to be returned and in the future such documents were not to be accepted. Furthermore, there were to be no discussions and that applied, not only to the Nuncio, it applied to all unauthorized actions particularly when foreign diplomats intervened in matters in which they had no jurisdiction.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: But do you want the Tribunal to understand that you went ahead and tried to do something about these complaints, whereas Ribbentrop did nothing; is that correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I tried to settle within my own sphere of jurisdiction all cases which, according to instructions, I was not permitted to accept at all. But if a case here and there was of primary importance, or where the lives of several people could have been saved, I always applied to Ribbentrop. In most of these cases Ribbentrop took the matter before Hitler, after we had invented a new competence, so that he could not raise the objection that the Nuncio had no jurisdiction. Upon this, Hitler either absolutely rejected them or at least said that the police would have to investigate the case first. This presented the grotesque picture that in a humanitarian matter or an affair which under all circumstances had to be handled as foreign politics, the Foreign Minister no longer made the decision, but the Criminal Inspector Meier or Schulze who only needed to state "Undesirable in the interests of state security."

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Did Ribbentrop obey the instructions which you say were received from the Fuehrer not to do anything about these complaints or did he not? "Yes" or "no"?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I cannot answer that question since I do not know how many orders he received from Hitler and whether he obeyed in each individual case.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, you have been testifying that you received instructions not to do anything about these complaints from the Vatican; is that not correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, and I did not obey them.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, I am now asking you whether Ribbentrop obeyed those instructions or whether he did not.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: But he was in a higher position. What orders Hitler gave to Ribbentrop privately I cannot say since I do not know.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Where did you receive your instructions from?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: From Ribbentrop.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Ribbentrop has testified under interrogation that he knew nothing of what went on in any of these concentration camps until the Fuehrer ordered Luther to be placed in a concentration camp. Do you know who Luther was?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Who was he, please?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Luther was an Under Secretary of State of the Foreign Office who was the head of the "Deutschland" department.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And when was he placed in a concentration camp?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: That must have been about February 1943.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Now, as a matter of fact, is it not true that Ribbentrop had a whole deskful of complaints from the Vatican about killings, atrocities, the starving of priests and nuns, to which he never made any reply at all, even an acknowledgment?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Mr. Prosecutor; what happened before May 1943, I do not know. As long as I was State Secretary, I never failed to accept a note or failed to answer it. On the contrary, I accepted all notes and attempted, as I said before, to assist these people. Regarding conditions before my term of service, I cannot give you any information because I do not know them.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, I am not talking about that time; I am talking about the period immediately before and following your appearance there in '43. Now I want to read you from ...

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I am sorry. I would gladly answer your question if I knew anything about the matter. During my time -- I cannot say anything about it because I do not know.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, I will read to you from the interrogation of Ribbentrop and ask you whether what he says conforms with your recollection of the facts.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I should, only like to say that until May 1943 I was not active politically, so that from my own knowledge I cannot make a statement about it.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, as I read the testimony to you, you will find that the interrogation refers to communications which remained in his desk unanswered for an indefinite period of time. Did you have access to Ribbentrop's desk? Did you knovr what was in it?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Does that conform with your recollection?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: That was, I must unfortunately say again, before my time. I cannot know whether he had a whole drawer full of things.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: If they had remained in his desk from March until May, then you would know about them; isn't that correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I? No. I was not Herr Ribbentrop's servant, who went over his chairs or drawers.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: So that your testimony is that you knew nothing about any protests from the Vatican other than those which you have already referred to?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Apart from those I have mentioned, I know nothing about protests. I emphasize again that during my time in office I accepted them all and answered them all.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: I will read you further from the interrogation: Does that conform with your recollection?

turns.speaker: VON SEEENGRACHT
turns.text: Certainly, that is correct. That was in accordance with the instructions which were originally given.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: By whom?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Hitler's instructions.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: To whom?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Certainly to Ribbentrop.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Those are the instructions which you say that you were violating on the side, is that correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Which I did not obey, for otherwise I would not have been allowed to accept the notes from the Vatican in all those cases where the jurisdiction was questioned; nor would I have been allowed to accept, for example, protests from the Swedish Ambassador regarding mistreatment in Norway, which, however, I also accepted.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: I will continue to read from the interrogation: Does that conform with your recollection?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: That Ribbentrop did not receive the protests any more? Yes, that is correct, that tallies with what I said, that in all these cases, where we could not accept them, I tried to settle them on my own responsibility, since it was against orders.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And in the course of reading these complaints from the Vatican which went unanswered, both you and Ribbentrop learned full details of exactly what was going on in the concentration camps, did you not?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: There was never anything about that in these notes -- the ones I saw -- there was never anything about the treatment in them. Instead they were concerned only with complaints asking why the death sentence was ever imposed, or why the clergyman was ever arrested, or similar cases, or the closing of churches or the like.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: I do not want to take the time of the Tribunal to read to you the documents which are already in evidence. I am referring to Document Numbers 3261-PS, 3262-PS, 3264-PS, 3267-PS, 3268-PS and 3269-PS, but in those documents -- I am sorry, sir, 3269 is not in evidence. But in those documents, Witness, are set forth the details of numerous individual and collective cases of just what went on in concentration camps. You say you were not familiar with any of those matters?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Mr. Prosecutor, I do not think that I expressed myself in that way. I gave you to understand that everything communicated to me by foreign diplomats I do, of course, know. In other words, if detailed reports were received during my term of office, then of course I know it. I never denied it.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What you said, Witness, was -- at least what I took down and understood you to say was -- that nothing was ever mentioned in the notes about the treatment in concentration camps.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: But I remarked with reference to the previous question, when the question was put generally as to whether I knew about conditions in concentration camps and the ill-treatment, I said that I knew everything that had been reported to me by foreign diplomats, by people of the opposition, and what I could learn from the foreign press. In other words, if these documents contained details during my time in office, then I know that too. But may I ask the date of the documents?

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: There are many documents with many dates, which can be obtained, but we don't want to take too much of the Tribunal's time. What I want to find out is whether or not you and Ribbentrop did not know all about the murders, tortures, starvations, and killings that were taking place in the concentration camps, and which were the subject of constant and continuous protests from the Vatican, which Ribbentrop has testified were not even read or acknowledged? Do you understand that, Witness?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I understand that. I knew nothing at all of the ill-treatment in concentration camps to the degree and in the bestial way that I have heard about here. I must strongly protest against the suggestion that I had heard things like that through the Vatican at that time. Also, I am convinced that Herr Von Ribbentrop had no idea of the details as we have heard them here and as'they have been shown in the films.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Isn't it a fact, Witness, that if you had followed up any of these complaints from the Vatican which Ribbentrop, has testified were ignored, you would have found out everything which was going on in the concentration camps to the last detail? "Yes" or "no."

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No, that is not correct. I said yesterday already that perhaps the key to it can be found in the speech made by Himmler on 3 October 1943, in which he said that the action against Jews and the matter of concentration camps were to be kept just as secret as the matter of 30 June 1934. And the great majority of the German people will confirm the fact that until a short time ago they could not discover anything at all about these events. If I went to GruppenFuehrer Muller or other officials I was always told that everything in those concentration camps was functioning beautifully and that there could be no question of illtreatment. Then I insisted that the foreigners, particularly the Red Cross, inspect a concentration camp, and the Danish Red Cross was taken to the Concentration Camp Theresienstadt. After that inspection took place -- this was a camp for Jews -- the Danish Minister came to me and told me that contrary to expectation everything had been favorable there. I expressed my astonishment and he told me, "Yes, our people were there, there was a theater there, and their own police force, their own hospital, their own money; the thing is well-run." I had no reason, therefore, to doubt that it was true. But I myself could get no idea of the true conditions from any German department, since they would certainly have been afraid to tell a member of the Foreign Office anything about it. But I want to emphasize again that we really had no idea of the atrocities and such things.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Why in the world should they be afraid to advise the Foreign Office of these atrocities? Had the Foreign Office ever done anything to discourage them?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: In all matters which were violations of international law we attempted to bring the case to the attention of the Red Cross in one way or another. We did this particularly in all matters relating to prisoners of war and if anything appeared to be wrong we drew the attention of the Swiss Delegate to it, on our own initiative: "Go to this place and see what is going on." And in this case too, if I had gone to the Swiss and told them in confidence that this and that has occurred in the concentration camps, Switzerland and the Red Cross would probably have interfered, which could ultimately have led to unpleasant measures.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Amen, I think we ought to have an adjournment for 10 minutes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: I have only a few more questions. [A recess was taken.]

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: So far as you know, after Ribbentrop had received this deskful of complaints from the Vatican, which he neither read nor acknowledged, did Ribbentrop take any steps or do anything to find out whether those complaints were justified and true, or did he not?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Regarding the complaints made before my time, I have no idea.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: I am asking you about any complaints that were received from the Vatican that ever came to your attention, with particular reference, of course, to the deskful to which Ribbentrop himself has testified. Do you know of any steps that were ever taken by Ribbentrop in connection with complaints received from the Vatican about the atrocities taking place in concentration camps? Please try to answer "yes" or "no."

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: So far as I recall he submitted complaints of this sort to Hitler, when he had the opportunity, and then waited for Hitler's order.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: All right. And when Hitler told him to pay no attention whatsoever to these complaints, he, as usual, did exactly what the Fuehrer told him to do, namely, nothing. Is that correct, so far as you know?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, he obeyed Hitler's orders.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And did nothing?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: If that is how the order read, he did nothing, yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, didn't you tell the Tribunal that is what the directive from the Fuehrer was, to pay no attention to these complaints? "Yes" or "no," please.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And so, I say, Ribbentrop, as usual, did nothing about any of these complaints after the Fuehrer instructed him to disregard them. Is that right?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I could not quite understand that question.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: I say after Ribbentrop received instructions from the Fuehrer to disregard these complaints from the Vatican, Ribbentrop, as usual, did what he was directed, namely, nothing.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I assume so, except for those cases where he nevertheless tried again and then received the same answer. I also know that he once appealed to Himmler and requested on principle that the actions against the Jews should not be carried out; and he proposed that Jewish children and women should, I believe, be turned over to England and America.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And you also know what reply he received to that suggestion, don't you?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I do not know the answer.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, you are certainly familiar with the fact that no such thing was ever done, are you not?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: That it was never carried out? I did not understand the question.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: The suggestion which you claim that Ribbentrop made to Himmler. That suggestion was never carried out, was it?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I do not understand; in what way not carried out? So far as I know -- Ribbentrop appealed directly to the foreign countries at that time. I also do not know what answer he received at that time, at least not in detail.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, so far as you know, nothing ever came of that suggestion, correct?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: No, nothing came of it.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And, as a matter of fact, you know that Ribbentrop and Himmler were not on good terms anyway, do you not?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: That was a matter of common knowledge to everybody, wasn't it?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, the enmity became greater in the course of time.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: So far as you know, did Ribbentrop take bromides every day?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: That I do not know. He...

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: You never saw him taking any?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: It could be; I do not know.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Well, did you ever see him taking any, or did he ever tell that he was taking them?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, I remember now that he took some sort of red substance but I did not pay particular attention to it.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do we have anything to do with whether he took bromides?

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Yes, your Lordship, we will, because in his interrogations he claims that his memory as to many of these events has been obscured or removed by the over-use of such medicine.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: All right.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Now, Witness, were you incarcerated at one time at a place known as "Ash Can"?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: In a refuse can?

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Outside of Luxembourg.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: In a refuse can? I cannot remember it.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Near Luxembourg.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Locked in a refuse can? No, I do not remember.

turns.speaker: COL.AMEN
turns.text: After you were taken prisoner, where were you incarcerated?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Mondorf.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: For how long a period of time?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: In Mondorf altogether 11 weeks.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And at that time were numerous of the defendants in this case also incarcerated there?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And while you were there you were free to have conversations with some of the inmates?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGBACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And you did, from time to time, have such conversations? Right?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes. I was not together with them all the time, because I was transferred to another camp.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Now, in the course of your conversations with one or another of the inmates there, did you make the statement which I am about to read to you, either in exact words or in substance? Do you understand the question? "Ribbentrop is lacking in any notion of decency and truth. The conception does not exist for him." Please answer "yes" or "no." Did you say that, Witness, did you say that?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I should be grateful if I could hear that exactly again what I am supposed to have said.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Now remember, I am asking you whether you said it either in the exact words or in substance. Do you understand that?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I did not precisely understand the German translation of your question.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Do you now understand it?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I do not understand. I did not exactly understand the German translation.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Yes, but do you understand my question, namely, that you are to say, whether you used these exact words or some other similar words? I will now read it to you again. Do you understand?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, I would be grateful.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: "Ribbentrop is lacking in any notion of decency and truth. The conception does not exist for him."

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I cannot recall that I ever made such statement. I would have to know to whom I am supposed to have said it.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Do you deny having made that statement, or is it simply that you can't remember whether you did or not?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I cannot remember having said that.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Is it possible that you did?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: It could be that I made such a statement in some connection.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Very good.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do the other prosecutors wish to ask any questions?

turns.speaker: MAJOR GENERAL N. D. ZORYA (Assistant Prosecutor for the U.S.S.R.)
turns.text: To save time, I shall restrict myself to a few question only. Insofar as I can understand the translation of your testimony which you submitted yesterday, you testified to the fact that besides the Ministry for Foreign Affairs many individuals and organization, had influenced Germany's foreign policy.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Tell me, which of the defendants in the present Trial whom you see in the dock attempted to influence and did, to a certain extent, influence Germany's foreign policy.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Foreign policy was, of course, after the beginning of the war...

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: I must ask you here and now not to make any declaration on Germany's foreign policy, but to indicate precisely, in the form of a reply to my question, which of the defendants in the present Trial attempted to influence and did influence Germany's foreign policy?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: The basic lines of foreign policy were determined solely by Hitler. The fact that we had occupied many countries and in these various countries had occupied the mos varied positions ...

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: We know all about that. I ask you to indicate by name, which of the defendants in the present Trial attempted to influence and did influence Germany's foreign policy. Is my question clear to you?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Foreign policy, as I stated yesterday, was in its broad outlines determined by Hitler alone; but those people who were assigned to special fields naturally exercised some influence in one respect or another. For example, some one who had a special assignment concerning the police, carried out police measures; some one who had to take care of labor problems conducted labor affairs. The same is true of other sectors.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: You still do not answer my question. I ask you to indicate, regardless of the form and extent of his influence, which of the defendants in the current Trial attempted to influence, and did influence, in one form or another, Germany's foreign policy, and this apart from representatives of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I assume that you are asking this question in relation to Russia; as the Foreign Office no longer had jurisdiction after the entrance of German troops into Russia ...

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: I request you to understand my question thoroughly and to answer which of the defendants, and in what form, regardless of concrete facts of foreign policy, attempted to influence this foreign policy of Germany and did, in effect, so influence it.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes. As regards Russia, the Eastern ministry was competent for these questions.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: No, not as regards Russia.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: In Norway Terboven laid down the policy. Quite naturally he influenced Hitler in his attitude toward Norway and Norwegian problems. In the same way the individual chiefs of the administrations in the individual countries exerted influence depending on how close they could come to Hitler with their reports.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: We don't want you to make speeches; we want you to answer the question. You weren't asked who influenced the foreign policy, but which of the defendants influenced foreign policy. You may say none, or you may say some. It is a question that you must be able to answer.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I would assume that Rosenberg had something to say regarding Russia, Frank had something to say regarding Poland, Seyss-Inquart had something to say regarding Holland. Other matters touched only special sectors. Naturally the SS had something to say; the Wehrmacht had something to say, also the various other offices and they naturally all exerted a certain influence but only a certain influence. However, the basic policy was conducted solely by Hitler.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Do you not wish in this connection to name the Defendant Goering?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Goering carried on the Four Year Plan and in this capacity he naturally also exercised a certain influence on Russia.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: What did this influence consist of?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: There again I must say that I and the Foreign Office had nothing to do with Russia, and that we were strictly forbidden to intervene in Russian affairs. In the sphere of propaganda and the press we were in no way permitted to become active. For this reason I am especially badly informed on Russian affairs.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Did the Defendant Goering have any influence in other questions besides the Russian question?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I did not understand the question in German.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Besides the Russian question, did the Defendant Goering exercise any influence on other questions in the sphere of foreign policy?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I would say that until the year 1938 he certainly had influence over Hitler in matters of foreign policy.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: You have stated in your testimony that in July '44 the Ministry for Foreign Affairs participated in preparations for the anti-Jewish Congress which, it was assumed, would be held in Krakow. Will you please answer this question briefly, "yes" or "no."

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Do you know who were the candidates for honorary membership in this congress?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Probably there were many, Ribbentrop among others, as far as I still remember today.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Who else from among the defendants?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I really cannot say. As far as I remember, Rosenberg and a large number of other leading personalities, but I cannot recall their names any longer. Naturally there are documents on the subject, so that it can be ascertained without trouble.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Did Ribbentrop attempt in any form whatsoever to protest against the inclusion of his name in the roster of honorary members of this congress?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: So far as I can recall he very unwillingly took over this post, but I do not believe that he really intended to take any active part in this matter.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: If I have understood you correctly, you have recently testified to the fact that relations between Ribbentrop and Himmler were hostile.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, bad relations.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: But can you state whether any contact existed between Ribbentrop and Himmler in their work, whether they maintained this contact in any one particular sphere or branch of their work?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: As a matter of fact, there was no working contact such as would have been considered right in a well-organized state. Of course, now and then there were matters somewhere that concerned both of these men, and to that extent they did have contact, yes.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: What was the nature of this contact, and what, exactly, did it represent?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: It really only amounted to this: that Ribbentrop or Himmler saw each other every few months. Besides that, we had a liaison man in the Foreign Office for the ReichsFuehrer SS Himmler.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Then how does all this fit in with the hostility which, as you have just mentioned, existed between Himmler and Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I presume you are referring to the second question I answered. In every normal state it was the case that the ministers saw each other at least once a year and exchanged opinions. This, however, did not take place, since, as we have already heard today at some length, the fields of jurisdiction overlapped to a great extent and the activity of one man touched very closely on the activity of the other. Therefore some connection had to be established whether one wanted it or not.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Do I understand you to say that Himmler and Ribbentrop never even met?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: They met perhaps once every 3 months. It might have been every 4 months and they usually met only if, by chance, both Ribbentrop and Himmler were visiting Hitler at the same time.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: And there were no special meetings, no business contact between them at all?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Actually not.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: I should like you to familiarize yourself with Document Number USSR-120, which has already been submitted as evidence to the Tribunal. You will see that this is an agreement between Himmler and Ribbentrop regarding the organization of intelligence work. Are you familiar with this agreement?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes, certainly.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: The contact between Himmler and Ribbentrop was evidently closer than you wished to describe.

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I do not believe, Mr. Prosecutor, that I wanted to give you any impression other than the one that actually existed. This refers to Hitler's order of 12 February 1944. On the basis of this order Himmler took charge of all activity abroad without the participation of the Foreign Office, and after he had become the successor to Canaris, through this order he secured a predominant position abroad. And if the Foreign Office in one way or another had not tried to contact this organization, then the Foreign Office would have had no influence at all even in foreign countries. We had to fight vigorously over this document, for on the basis of this document Himmler wasobliged for the first time to communicate to us also the information that he brought to Germany. Otherwise he brought these reports in without telling us about them. That was the reason why we reached this working agreement. But so far as I recall, it was not put into practice at all, because Hitler's order was issued on 12 February 1944 and we had not come to an agreement until February 1945. Then it gradually came about. That must be approximately the date. At any rate it took quite a while.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: You say that this agreement never became valid?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: I did not say that. An agreement becomes effective at the moment in which it is signed. But it was not put into practice or hardly put into practice.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: I think we shall have to content ourselves with your reply and pass over to some other questions. Did you ever come in contact with Kaltenbrunner ?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Did I come into contact with Kaltenbrunner? Yes.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: On what questions?

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: On precisely those questions which, for example, the Nuncio addressed to me and also about people who because of the Nacht und Nebel decree had been deported from abroad and about whom we were not allowed to give information, I often went privately to Kaltenbrunner and pointed out to him that this order was inhuman. As a favor Kaltenbrunner then frequently gave me information; and I, contrary to the orders, transmitted this information abroad because I considered it justified for humanity's sake. Those were the main points of contact which I had with Kaltenbrunner.

turns.speaker: GEN. ZORYA
turns.text: Did you, in particular, have any conversation with him on the subject of the Danish policemen interned by the Gestapo,in a concentration camp without any concrete charges presented against them? Please reply to this question by saying "yes" or "no."

turns.speaker: VON STEENGRACHT
turns.text: Yes.
